paper_title: Speculative Decoding for Autoregressive Video Generation

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Autoregressive video diffusion is emerging as a promising paradigm for streaming video synthesis, with step distillation serving as the primary means of accelerating inference. Whether speculative decoding, the dominant acceleration strategy for large language models, can be effectively adapted to autoregressive video generation remains an open question, because video blocks are continuous spatiotemporal tensors with no token-level distribution for exact rejection sampling. We introduce SDVG, which brings speculative decoding to block-based autoregressive video diffusion by replacing token verification with an image-quality router. A 1.3B drafter proposes candidate blocks via four denoising steps; each block is VAE-decoded and scored by ImageReward using worst-frame aggregation--taking the minimum per-frame reward to catch single-frame artifacts that averaging would mask. Blocks scoring above a fixed threshold tau are accepted into the 14B target's KV cache; the rest are regenerated by the target. Two additional design choices prove critical: the first block is always force-rejected to anchor scene composition, and tau serves as a single knob that traces a smooth quality-speed Pareto frontier. On 1003 MovieGenVideoBench prompts (832x480), SDVG retains 98.1% of target-only VisionReward quality (0.0773 vs. 0.0788) at a 1.59x speedup with tau=-0.7, and reaches 2.09x at 95.7% quality retention--while consistently outperforming draft-only generation by over +17%. The framework is training-free, requires no architectural changes, and can be seamlessly integrated into existing autoregressive video generation pipelines.

sections.title:
Speculative Decoding for Autoregressive Video
Generation
sections.content: Yuezhou Hu University of California, Berkeley yuezhouhu@berkeley.edu Jintao Zhang University of California, Berkeley jintaozhang@berkeley.edu

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Autoregressive video diffusion is emerging as promising paradigm for streaming video synthesis, with step distillation serving as the primary means of accelerating inference. Whether speculative decoding, the dominant acceleration strategy for large language models, can be effectively adapted to autoregressive video generation remains an open question, because video blocks are continuous spatiotemporal tensors with no token-level distribution for exact rejection sampling. We introduce SDVG, which brings speculative decoding to block-based autoregressive video diffusion by replacing token verification with an image-quality router. 1.3B drafter proposes candidate blocks via four denoising steps; each block is VAE-decoded and scored by ImageReward using worst-frame aggregationtaking the minimum per-frame reward to catch single-frame artifacts that averaging would mask. Blocks scoring above fixed threshold τ are accepted into the 14B targets KV cache; the rest are regenerated by the target. Two additional design choices prove critical: the first block is always force-rejected to anchor scene composition, and τ serves as single knob that traces smooth qualityspeed Pareto frontier. On 1003 MovieGenVideoBench prompts (832480), SDVG retains 98.1% of target-only VisionReward quality (0.0773 vs. 0.0788) at 1.59 speedup with τ =0.7, and reaches 2.09 at 95.7% quality retentionwhile consistently outperforming draft-only generation by over +17%. The framework is training-free, requires no architectural changes, and can be seamlessly integrated into existing autoregressive video generation pipelines.

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: Autoregressive video generation has recently emerged as compelling paradigm for efficient, streaming video synthesis. Unlike conventional video diffusion models that generate all frames jointly [1, 10], autoregressive approaches produce video block by block, conditioning each new block on previously generated content through shared key-value (KV) cachemirroring the autoregressive paradigm of large language models (LLMs). This design eliminates exposure bias and enables streaming generation: frames can be displayed as they are produced, rather than waiting for the full sequence to complete. Self-Forcing [5] exemplifies this approach, training causal video diffusion transformer with self-generated conditioning that achieves real-time video output on single GPU. Despite this structural efficiency advantage, state-of-the-art autoregressive video models are built on 10B+ parameter transformers, which is still computationally demanding. For example, frontier open-source 14B autoregressive video generation models (such as Millon [8]) require high-end GPUs (e.g. NVIDIA B200) to achieve real-time throughput. Meanwhile, compact 1B-scale video models, such as Wan et al. [12], run at less than one-quarter of the computational cost but produce lower but still reasonable quality. Thus, the central question is then: *Equal Contribution. Corresponding Author. Preprint. Figure 1: Qualitative comparison on MovieGenVideoBench. Each row shows 4 uniformly sampled frames from generated video. Draft-only (left) is fast but produces lower-fidelity output; Target-only (right) achieves the highest quality at the cost of slow inference. SDVG (center) closely matches target quality while running 1.59 faster. Prompts: (top) Drone view of waves crashing against the rugged cliffs along Big Sur. . . ; (bottom) stylish woman walks down Tokyo street. . . . Can we capture the speed of small models while retaining the quality of large ones? Speculative decoding for LLMs [6, 2] offers compelling blueprint: small draft model proposes candidate outputs, and the large target model is invoked only when necessary. The block-by-block structure of autoregressive video generation is especially well-suited to this paradigmeach generated block is self-contained unit that can be evaluated before being committed to the KV cache, making per-block routing natural design choice. Making large and small models cooperate effectively is, however, non-trivial. Recent work has explored related ideas. T-Stitch [9], SRDiffusion [3], and HybridStitch [11] all propose splitting the denoising trajectory between models at the noise-step level: the small model handles certain steps and the large model handles others. MoDM [14] routes entire generation requests between models at the serving system level based on caching mechanism. While effective in their respective settings, these approaches were not designed for autoregressive video generation and carry meaningful limitations. T-Stitch, SRDiffusion, and HybridStitch use fixed step splits without detecting or correcting poor drafts, while MoDM relies on cache hits and lacks per-block quality guarantees. All four require extra trajectory or system-level engineering, increasing deployment complexity. further challenge distinguishes video from LLMs: classical speculative decoding accepts or rejects drafts via exact token-probability comparisons [6]. Video blocks are continuous, high-dimensional spatiotemporal tensors with no associated logit distribution, making token-level verification inapplicable. This leaves speculative generation for autoregressive video as an open problem. In this work, we propose Speculative Decoding for Autoregressive Video Generation (SDVG), training-free, plug-and-play framework that requires no architectural changes to either the drafter or target. For each video block, the drafter generates candidate; an image quality router then decides, per block, whether to accept the draft or invoke the target for regeneration. Our key insight is that plain image-quality signal, applied block-by-block with fixed threshold, is sufficient to match target-only quality without any step-level trajectory engineeringsimplicity is feature, not limitation. On 1003 MovieGenVideoBench prompts at 832480 resolution, SDVG achieves 98.1% of target-only VisionReward quality (0.0773 vs. 0.0788) at 1.59 speedup. Importantly, SDVG is orthogonal to step-level methods such as T-Stitch or SRDiffusion, which can be directly applied to the target models generation steps within SDVG, providing composable path to further speedup. Our main contributions are as follows: We propose SDVG, training-free speculative generation framework for autoregressive video diffusion that routes each block between drafter and target based on image quality routing, achieving 1.59 speedup with 98.1% quality retention. Figure 2: SDVG inference pipeline. For each video block, the 1.3B drafter generates candidate in 4 denoising steps. Block 0 is always force-rejected to anchor scene composition. For blocks 18, the draft is VAE-decoded and scored by ImageReward using min-frame aggregation (qb = mini R(fi, p)). If qb τ , the draft is accepted (blue) and committed to the target KV cache; otherwise the 14B target regenerates the block (orange). We identify three video-specific design choices critical to making reward-guided routing effective: fixed ImageReward threshold that provides simple, calibration-free quality speed knob, mandatory first-block regeneration to anchor scene composition, and worstframe quality scoring to surface single-frame artifacts masked by block averages. We demonstrate that without any complex step-level trajectory engineering, plain reward routing signal suffices to match large-model qualityestablishing new, simpler baseline for collaborative video generation.

sections.title: 2 Background
sections.content: Video generation. Diffusion-based video models have advanced from pixel-space approaches [4] to large latent transformer architectures [1, 10]. Inference efficiency has been improved primarily through step distillation [17, 24, 13] and GPU kernel optimization [21, 18, 23, 20, 22, 19]. They are all orthogonal to SDVG. Autoregressive video generation. Autoregressive video generation models future video blocks causally based on previously generated content. Early training methods rely on ground-truth history during training, resulting in mismatch with autoregressive inference and consequently causing exposure bias. More recent methods, such as Self-Forcing [5], aim to improve traininginference consistency in block-wise video generation. Hierarchical video generation. T-Stitch [9], SRDiffusion [3], and HybridStitch [11] split the denoising trajectory or space between small and large model at fixed noise levels, achieving training-free acceleration. MoDM [14] routes entire requests to smaller models on cache hits, reducing average serving time by 2.5. However, these methods apply content-agnostic step-level splits and were not designed for block-level autoregressive video. Speculative decoding. Speculative decoding [6, 2] pairs small drafter with large target: the drafter proposes tokens and the target verifies them in one pass, preserving the target distribution exactly. RSD [7] lifts acceptance to the reasoning-step level using Process Reward Model (PRM), 3 directly inspiring SDVG. The key difference is that video blocks are continuous tensors with no token distribution, so exact rejection sampling is inapplicable; we replace the PRM with an image quality model as block-level proxy.

sections.title: 3 Method: SDVG
sections.content: Given text prompt p, drafter and target , we seek routing policy π : {0, 1} that maps per-block quality score qb to accept (1) or reject (0), optimizing the trade-off between video quality and inference speed. Inference flow. For each block b, runs denoising steps to produce candidate ˆxb. The drafter KV cache KD is updated unconditionally, ensuring always conditions on its own prior outputs. The candidate is decoded by the VAE and scored by the router. If qb τ , the draft is accepted: ˆxb is committed to the targets KV cache KT and the decoded frames are emitted directly. If rejected, , updating KT . The VAE decode runs denoising steps from the same initial noise to produce cache is cloned before draft scoring and restored on rejection to prevent temporal inconsistency across blocks. In SDVG the threshold τ is fixed scalar calibrated offline. Worst-frame aggregation. The block quality score is the minimum per-frame reward over the decoded frames: qb = min i=1 (cid:16) (b) R (cid:17) , . (1) Here R(f , p) denotes the reward of single decoded frame given prompt p. Using the minimum rather than the mean catches blocks with one severely degraded framea visual artifact that average scoring would mask. Force-reject the first block. Block = 0 is always regenerated by , regardless of its draft score. Block 0 lacks any KV context from prior blocks and establishes the scene composition, foreground subjects, and visual style that all subsequent blocks inherit through the shared cache. Accepting draft at this position risks propagating irreversible layout errors throughout the video.

sections.title: 4 Experiments
sections.content: 4.1 Experimental Setup Models. We evaluate SDVG on pair of autoregressive video diffusion models built on the Wan2.1 architecture [12]. The target model is Krea Realtime Video 14B [8], distilled from Wan2.1-T2V-14B via Self-Forcing [5]. The drafter is the original Wan2.1-T2V-1.3B Self-Forcing model. Both models share the same causal attention backbone with KV caching via RoPE positional embeddings, and run 4 denoising steps per block using the schedule = [1000, 937, 833, 625, 0] in bfloat16 precision (guidance scale 3.0, timestep shift 5.0). The reward router uses ImageReward [15], an off-the-shelf text-image reward model, to score decoded draft frames. Generation protocol. Each video consists of B=9 autoregressive blocks. Each block corresponds to 3 latent frames (27 latent frames in total). The causal VAE decoder produce 9 pixel frames for the first video block and 12 pixel frames per later video block at 832 480 resolution. All runs use fixed random seed (42) for reproducibility. The routing threshold is set to τ =0.7 (min-frame ImageReward) unless otherwise noted. Hardware and implementation. All experiments are conducted on two NVIDIA RTX A6000 GPUs (48 GB each). GPU 0 hosts the diffusion transformer (both target and drafter); GPU 1 hosts the text encoder (UMT5-XXL), causal VAE, and ImageReward. CUDA streams overlap cross-device transfers with compute so that reward scoring does not block denoising. The VAE decode cache is cloned before draft scoring and restored upon rejection to preserve temporal consistency across blocks. Evaluation benchmarks and metrics. We draw prompts from MovieGenVideoBench [10], which spans diverse categories including landscapes, animals, human activities, and cinematic footage. We report results on the full 1003-prompt set. Video quality is measured by VisionReward [16], Table 1: Main results on 1003 MovieGenVideoBench prompts (832480, 9 blocks/video). VR = VisionReward (higher is better). Time = average wall-clock time per video. Speedup is relative to target-only. Accept rate excludes block 0 (always force-rejected).

sections.title: Accept
sections.content: 97.0 60.9 58.6 58.3 57.2 51.6 47.4 46.4 25.7 1.00 1.59 1.66 1.66 1.69 1.88 2.05 2.09 3.77 73.1% 74.9% 76.4% 78.0% 83.4% 87.5% 88.9% VQA-based metric that aggregates 29 questions covering visual quality, temporal consistency, motion naturalness, and textvideo alignment. Efficiency is measured by wall-clock time per video (excluding model loading and warmup), and the resulting speedup relative to target-only generation. Baselines. We compare against two boundary baselines: Draft-onlyall blocks generated by the 1.3B drafter (maximum speed, lowest quality); Target-onlyall blocks generated by the 14B target (minimum speed, highest quality). SDVG operates between these extremes by selectively routing blocks. 4.2 Main Results Table 1 presents SDVG results across sweep of fixed min-frame thresholds on 1003 MovieGenVideoBench prompts, alongside the two boundary baselines. Qualitytime tradeoff. By sweeping the threshold τ from 0.7 to 2.5, SDVG traces smooth Pareto frontier between the two baselines (Figure 3). At the conservative end (τ =0.7), SDVG retains 98.10% of target-only VisionReward (0.0773 vs. 0.0788) with 1.59 speedup. As the threshold relaxes, inference time continues to drop: τ =1.0 pushes the speedup to 1.69 while preserving 96.95% of target quality. At the aggressive end (τ =2.5), SDVG reaches 2.09 speedup at 95.69% quality retentionstill substantially above draft-only (0.0754 vs. 0.0644, +17.1% gap). The diminishing returns beyond τ =1.5 suggest that most quality-critical blocks already have ImageReward scores above 1.5; further relaxation buys little additional speed. Qualityacceptance rate tradeoff. An alternative view of the same frontier is the relationship between VisionReward and draft acceptance rate. As the accept rate rises from 73.1% (τ =0.7) to 78.0% (τ =1.0), VisionReward decreases only marginally (0.0773 0.0764, 0.12% absolute). This near-flat region indicates that the additional drafts admitted by relaxing τ are borderline cases whose quality is close to the target models outputthe reward-guided router selectively accepts drafts that would not degrade perceptual quality. Beyond 78% acceptance, quality begins to drop more noticeably (0.0757 at 83.4%, 0.0754 at 88.9%), as increasingly low-scoring drafts are admitted. The inflection around τ [1.0, 0.7] (7378% acceptance) identifies the operating regime where SDVG delivers the most favorable qualityefficiency balance. 4.3 Ablation Studies We ablate the two key design choices in SDVGthe scoring strategy and the routing signalto isolate their contributions. Table 2 reports results on 1003 MovieGenVideoBench prompts. All ablations use the same force-reject-block-0 policy and fixed thresholds for controlled comparison. Reward-guided vs. random routing. To verify that the ImageReward signal is load-bearing, we replace the reward router with random accept/reject decisions (at matched overall accept rate). 5 Figure 3: Qualityspeed Pareto curve. SDVG (τ =0.7) achieves 98.1% of target quality at 1.59 speedup, operating between draft-only and target-only. Table 2: Ablation study. 1003 MovieGenVideoBench prompts, 832480, 9 blocks/video. VR = VisionReward (higher is better). Time = average wall-clock time per video. Accept rate excludes block 0 (always force-rejected). indicates not applicable. Method Target-only SDVG (τ =0.7, ours) VR 0.0788 0.0773 Avg-frame scoring (replacing min-frame) Avg-frame τ =0.2 Avg-frame τ =0.5 Avg-frame τ =0.7 0.0767 0.0759 0.0755 Routing signal ablation Force-reject + random routing Random routing Draft-only 0.0771 0.0706 0.0644 Time (s) Speedup Accept 97.0 60.9 63.2 59.0 56.9 58.2 60.2 25.7 1.00 1.59 1.54 1.64 1.71 1.67 1.61 3.77 73.1% 70.2% 75.3% 78.4% 70.3% 70.0% Random routing yields VisionReward 0.0706, sharp drop from the reward-guided SDVG (0.0773 at τ =0.7) and even below the force-reject-block-0 baseline (0.0757 from the main table). Without quality signal, the router accepts artifact-heavy blocks and rejects clean ones with equal probability, negating the benefit of selective regeneration. Min-frame vs. average-frame scoring. Our default SDVG uses min-frame aggregation (Eq. 1), which flags blocks where even single frame is degraded. Replacing it with average-frame scoring consistently underperforms: at comparable accept rates, the avg-frame variants achieve lower VisionReward (e.g., 0.0755 at 78.4% acceptance vs. 0.0773 at 73.1% for min-frame τ =0.7). This confirms that averaging masks per-frame artifactsa single corrupted frame among =3 produces visible temporal flickering that the mean score fails to catch, leading the router to accept low-quality blocks.

sections.title: 5 Limitations
sections.content: Distributional bias. Unlike exact-rejection LLM speculative decoding, SDVG accepts distributional shift toward the drafter. lower accept rate (stricter threshold) reduces the gap at the cost of speedup. ImageReward as proxy. ImageReward was trained on text-image pairs and evaluates frames independently, missing temporal consistency and motion quality. dedicated video-block quality model would improve the routing signal. 6 Wasted draft computation. For rejected blocks (including forced block-0 rejections), the drafter forward pass and VAE decode are wasted computation. Batching or speculative VAE decoding could reduce this overhead.

sections.title: 6 Conclusion
sections.content: We presented SDVG, reward-guided speculative video generation framework for autoregressive video diffusion that achieves 98.1% of target quality at 1.59 speedup. The key design choicesforced first-block regeneration and worst-frame quality scoringaddress the specific challenges of blocklevel video speculative decoding. single fixed threshold provides simple qualityspeed knob: sweeping it from 0.7 to 2.5 traces smooth Pareto frontier reaching up to 2.09 speedup while remaining well above draft-only quality. SDVG can be applied to any Self-Forcing-style autoregressive video model with drafter-target pair, opening the door to reward-guided inference-time compute allocation for video generation.
